guest_role: Founder, CEO and Chairman of Farfetch
assertiveness_why: Harry asks thoughtful, layered follow-up questions and even one 'potentially divisive' question about stubbornness, but he never pushes back, contests a claim, or plants an adversarial thesis — the tone is exploratory and receptive throughout.
question_style: Open, philosophical, and personal in tone, often following the guest's own language back to him (e.g. picking up 'detachment' and 'right action'); Harry shares his own vulnerability (self-doubt) rather than challenging the guest, and there's no interruption or contestation.
tone: Reflective, warm, and unusually personal/vulnerable for the format — Harry frames this as 'a very real conversation about what truly great leadership means' and opens up about his own self-doubt.
depth: Goes deep into philosophy and psychology (Zen Buddhism, Antonio Damasio's neuroscience of emotion and decision-making, detachment during a stock crash) more than operational business detail; less technical/strategic and more introspective than typical episodes.
harry_quotes: Do you think the ability to detach comes from bluntly financial security?
Can I ask one that's potentially divisive?
How do you deal with that loneliness today?
learnings: Neves treats Zen practice as continuous awareness of the present moment rather than a discrete meditation session, arguing it can happen even mid-activity (e.g. on a Peloton).

He cites Antonio Damasio's neuroscience research to argue that emotion and rational thought are not separable in good decision-making — patients who lose emotional processing but retain logic make catastrophic real-world decisions.
During Farfetch's stock being caught in the Archegos Capital unwind, he describes handling a 20% intraday drop with detachment rather than panic, framing it as proof he was never in control anyway.

He reframes self-doubt as a healthy, protective mental construct rather than something to eliminate — the same mental capacity that generates a negative scenario can be redirected to generate a positive one.
His approach to industry dealmaking is built around a 'win-win-win' ecosystem view (illustrated via the Off-White/Louis Vuitton/Virgil Abloh story) rather than zero-sum negotiation.
notable: Unusually introspective, philosophy-heavy episode — first time Harry says he's had a Zen Buddhist guest, and he shares his own struggle with self-doubt rather than only asking about the guest's.
